speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the effects Hitler had on your family's life. Can you start-- can you remember some of the earliest signs of Nazism that you witnessed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I know that my brothers got in trouble for talking to German girls. And they were warned not to talk to them. But the girls went after them. I had handsome brothers. And one got arrested once. And both of them became pretty distraught about life in Germany. And Werner decided to move. Werner decided to go to Palestine. And he was the first to leave in 1934. I was four years old at the time. And of course, we missed him. And we went to visit him when he got married in 1938, quite late on. But we were still running our business. Life hadn't changed that much. And it was before Kristallnacht.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Israel. And Israel-- he lived in Herzliya, which was very primitive at the time. It was all orange groves and dirt roads. And I remember there was a string of dogs, wild dogs, running through the town. And they called the leader of the pack Hitler.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought that was really funny, because Hitler was already becoming known as an autocratic, destructive force in the world-- OK. Yes, I made friends with a little Arab boy that talked to me through the fence. My brother Werner was in the Haganah. He fought the Arabs very early on. And he had a beautiful bride. Her name is Ruth also. And I was their flower girl at their wedding. And then we went back to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you hesitant to go back?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can't say I was, at eight years old, no. I just did whatever my mama did. But Mama had her business to run. She had many employees that were depending on her. And that was our livelihood.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember most of that trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip? The trip itself-- I enjoyed the boat. I did get sea sick. Mama got much more seasick than I did. I just saw the ship going like this and like that. And then when I gave back my food, I just went and ate some more. I did meet-- my-- my father had a brother that lived in Israel. I met an uncle for the first time. My family was already split up quite a bit, the extended family. I didn't see too much of them. So I met some new relations there. And of course, they got to meet my new sister-in-law and her family. They had come from Germany, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other changes were going on back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back home, in 1938--

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you seen the Hitler Youth?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw the Hitler Youth. I was not allowed to be part of it, naturally. I was barred from joining the children singing and dancing. And I kind of thought it looked like fun. I watched them from my window. And we had a little balcony on that first floor. And we were across from the marketplace, where all their little get togethers took place. Then, in the book that the archivers gave me in Leverkusen when I went back, there's a story about the little Jewish girl-- and they named me, Ruth Heumann, with black hair, and my hair was quite black, and green-eyed. And this little girl was feeling sad and crying because she couldn't play with the children in the Hitlerjugend. And this gentleman-- I don't recall his name, but it's in the book-- took me by the hand. And he said, Ruth, it's all right for you to join the children. And the children took me in. But that was just that one time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any anti-Semitic incidents that happened while you were in school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never. Never. I never had any anti-Semitism-- not even a word that I was different from any of my schoolmates. They were all polite little children. And my teacher adored me, and I adored her. Frau Gunther was her name. And the law was passed after Kristallnacht that German teachers were not to teach Jewish children any longer. And we heard that-- my mother and my future sister-in-law heard that on the radio. And immediately, my mother sent my future sister-in-law, Dina, to pick me up at school, to take me out. And I remember Dina coming. And Dina and Frau Gunther were talking. And Frau Gunther was very distraught and very upset. And she said, I'm not going to let Ruth go-- she's my good student. She's one of my best. I don't want her to leave. And Dina said, you have no choice. And Dina took me home. Mrs. Gunther was very, very kind, wonderful teacher. Before I left Germany, she sent two beautiful books to me, picture books, and with a dedication in them for me. Unfortunately, I was unable to carry them. We had only so much that we could carry when we actually left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was never any Nazi ideologies taught at your school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not in those grades at that time, now. But however, in the community, there were big Nazi parades, lots of those red flags with the swastikas hanging from the Haupstrasse, which is the main street, right around the corner from where we lived. And my school was on that street. And I had to walk on that street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when those parades were taking place-- now, my mother told me, Jewish children may not salute the flag. However, there's a law that says anyone on the street must salute the flag. So I was caught in a dilemma. What do I do? I'm damned either way. So I hid in stairwells and storefronts. And I hid so I wouldn't have to be seen. Or to salute the flag. That was very traumatic. And I have a real strong recollections of the street, the noise of the goose-stepping Nazis, and the frenzy that was going on. I do recall that very clearly. What kind of emotion did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fear. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Nothing else. I was too young to be angry. This was a given. This was the way my life was. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you wonder why you were made to be different from the other kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother said, we were Jewish. But it's OK to be Jewish. This is what I knew from my mother. The consequences didn't hit me, yet. I mean, I was feeling them, yes. I was left out of things. But this is just-- first of all, my mother had a certain fatalism in her attitude. She knew that, throughout history, Jews were persecuted. And sooner or later-- it was already happening here-- that it would not only happen, it would get worse and worse. My brother Alfred informed her one morning that he was going to leave Germany, because there was no future there for him. He had brought home his fiancee, Hildegard Kohn, who lived in another town. She came to visit us. We met her, and she was a very beautiful woman. And then the next thing we knew, Alfred was planning to go to America, because her mother was going to sponsor Alfred so he could get in. There was no quota yet-- or at least he could-- he had time to go. This was in 1937.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Alfred, who was my mother's right hand in the store, left. And that left Helmut and Joe to help her with the store. Can you tell me what you remember of the night of Kristallnacht in 1938?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We lived upstairs of the store. And during the night, there was a huge crash. The Nazis threw bricks into the store windows and destroyed the whole-- all the glass. There were three walls of glass that came tumbling down. And we shook. And I don't know what the adults were saying. They sent me to bed. And the next morning, my brothers were taken into, quote, protective custody, into the local jail for being Jews. While they were there, my mother was allowed to bring them food. And she did that. And also, my future sister-in-law, Dina, did that, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: While my brothers were in there. One of the people, the Germans that worked in the jail, gave my brother Helmut some advice. He said, if I were you, I would tell your mother to go to Holland, or send your fiancee to Holland, and get you passages to another country. You've got to get out. You have to save your life. OK? So he had that knowledge. They let them out of this jail for a couple of days. They came home. And they didn't know what was going to happen to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: But within a few days, they took all the Jewish men in town. They didn't take the families. They separated the families. They took the males first, male-- the adult males. And Joe and Helmut were taken away. And again, they were told it was protective custody. In the meantime, after Kristallnacht, we also found out Joe and Helmut got into their cars and went to visit the local-- the relatives that were close by.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before they were sent away.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before they were arrested, right. They had been looking for them when they were gone. But they made these rounds, and came back to tell my mother the horrifying tales-- that two old ladies that were widows that lived together in a small little farm town, who had just finished putting up all their preserves for the winter, their food, the Nazis came into their house, smashed all the food, destroyed it, and poured gasoline over it. They did not set it on fire. But they destroyed all their food. And then they came to another place where an uncle-- whose antiques that I have with me today that my mother took to this country, some of the antique little pieces that he had in his ancient little home-- he lived by himself. They took him outside, took him from bed and beat him to death outside of his home. And these were the horror stories that the adults were talking about. They didn't want me to know these things. But I would listen. I was curious. And I would hear those things. It was very terrifying.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your synagogue? Did you find that what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I found out our synagogue, all the synagogues, were attacked that night. The one in Opladen was burned. People saw it burning. And they wanted to put it out. But they were told they couldn't. And the Nazis made it burn to the ground. It was totally destroyed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of why this night of violence occurred?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody could understand it, except that it was Hitler and his gung ho seizure of the people and the country and the wild speeches he made that you heard on the radio. There was no freedom of speech, which we, in this country, don't understand. It's something that you can't explain very easily. It's just-- you know, the rights weren't there. People didn't have the same rights that we have here. Now, the woman that worked for us, her name was Gertrude. She was a devoted older lady. She adored me. She loved my cat. My cat became my only companion in those years. And I developed a tremendously strong affection for Prince. And he filled that void. I put him in my doll bed and put dolls clothes on Prince. I wheeled him in a buggy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Gertrude had to vote. And there was no one to vote for except Hitler. You know? She had to vote. And she didn't want to vote. And my mother said, Gertrude, do what you're supposed to do, or you will lose your life. I remember that conversation.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then the fears were growing, the rumors about Jews going to ghettos. I had never heard that word before. And Aunt Martha and Mom explained that to me to me, and that maybe someday we would go. And they were very frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you find out where your brothers were sent?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't hear from them for a while. Then we did hear that they were in Dachau. But again, their mail was restricted. And they didn't know of each other's whereabouts when they were there. But Mom sent Dina to Holland. And Dina tried to get passages to another country. She tried Paraguay, Uruguay. And that didn't work. They were willing to go anywhere. She finally got a passage to Peru. And she begged Mom, please let me go along, because if I don't, I'll never see Helmut again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this is your future sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my future sister-in-law. They did get their passages. They got married on the ship by the captain. And they went to Peru. They had a lot of hardships there. They had to work in a Coca-Cola bottling factory. That's the only work they could get when they came. And later on, Joe tried to get work as a painter, which is the one skill that he had, and he would fall off the ladder, because there were so many earthquakes. So Dina and Helmut were starting a family, and he decided to come to America, where Alfred was working on getting us out. So Joe went to America. And in the meantime, Mom worked on getting out. We got a number, which was never called, an immigration number.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your mother arrange for the two of you to leave Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a very close family friend. The name was Kindervater. He was a non-Jew, a German non-Jew. And he was married to a Jewish woman. He was in the export business. And he had some good connections. So he came up with some wonderful ideas. He said, you can go to England. We'll get you somehow to England. So we started packing-- all our things were packed. Alfred wrote from America. He was keeping on working, and if we came through England, that would be fine. We should send a lot of our things-- as much as we could get out. And Mother bought a lift, which is the size of a small room. And she put in furniture. Alfred said, don't send the modern things, we can get those here. Sends Dad's antiques. So he packed-- she packed all those things. She packed linens, she packed towels, she packed clothes for me that she had made for me. And everything was put into trunks, put into the lift-- china-- and off it went. But we didn't go. We were there for two more years. And one thing-- this was a time when you just never knew what would happen next. Every day, the laws changed. The Jews' rights were just twiddling away. We couldn't appear in public places. Mother did take me to plays in Cologne, because she wanted me to see Hansel and Gretel, the opera. And she wanted to take me places, but she risked going there. Because it was a big city, and we wouldn't be detected, we got away with it. But she couldn't keep on doing that. It became too nerve-wracking. We could not appear any place in public anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear armbands?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet. Not yet. That came a little bit later. Like I said, these things developed days at a time. But they kept on developing. It was about me not being able to go to school anymore, and then the non-fraternization. Just every day, some new laws were passed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to continue to own your business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We had to sell. I think I mentioned earlier that we sold to the Poles, which were Catholic people that Mother respected, and vice versa, and they were very good to us. And later on, during the war years, they would share rations with us, and risked getting in trouble. And I was not allowed to go to school anymore. And I mentioned how that happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I started to go to school in a private Jewish school in Cologne, which was on public transportation, streetcars, which took at least an hour. And of course, the war started with England. And while the war was going on, here was this nine-year-old girl traveling by herself from one city to another.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came across flashers. I came across-- there were air raids when I got caught. I had to get off the-- the streetcar. And I had to disappear into some air raid shelter. And I didn't talk to anyone, because I didn't want anyone to know I was Jewish, because then they would have kicked me out. And Mother didn't know where I was. I would go to school and come home late. And the Jewish school was wonderful. The children were nice. The teacher was nice. But they were ahead of me in Hebrew and in math. So I never caught up. And I had no one to help me with the work at home. Aunt Martha was already back with Grandmother. So I had no one to help me. It was a pretty rough time-- pretty rough. But we just-- you just do what you have to do. You continue on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there food rations for the Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: When the war started, eventually yes. There were rations that the Germans were rationed. We did not get the ration stamps. We weren't allowed to get any food. We could buy things like eel and some bread. But we couldn't have the rationed foods, like milk and sugar and good staples.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Mother was used to hardships, because in World War I, when my dad got wounded, she was home with four little boys, and they had no food. She would make these [GERMAN], which means one-- one dish menus, which would be consist of a bean soup or lentil soup, and she put a piece of sausage in if she had it. And she would just make something out of nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so she again did that, with the same courage and bravery she had before. So I was not starving. But whenever we got something good, it was such a treat that I told the children in school the next day, I had German bread. We shared rations with a soldier.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't get in trouble, because who were the Jewish children gonna tell? But when I told my Mother I told the children, she became livid. She said, oh my God, what did you do? You'll get us all killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: You get the Poles killed for sharing the bread. You can't do this. You can't talk. Try and tell a nine-year-old kid not to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: I know what that is today being a mom, OK? But it was rough at the time. And I developed quite a complex, guilt complex. You can't do this. You can't do that. You'd better be good. You think that it affected your personality at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think so. I think so. I did a lot of crying when I was a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living in constant fear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the fear. And also, even in this country, when I experienced things that weren't-- and I had some prejudice that I experienced in this country. It hit me right in the stomach. And whenever I heard a siren go off, I felt like I did in Germany, living in the maid's quarters without a bathroom. What happened is that the British came over 56 nights in a row the last summer we were there. Because we counted-- we had nothing else to do. They came over and bombed every night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mom and I were happy the Germans were getting bombed, OK? But we were right in there. And the bombs did hit close to home. One right, the sirens went off. And this was this huge noise. It would wake up the dead, OK? And we lived in an apartment community, where there were lots of apartments. When the sirens quieted down, there was complete stillness. And then you heard all the toilets flushing-- lots of nervous stomachs.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I developed, too. And we didn't have a toilet. And during the night, we couldn't use the Poles' toilet. They had let us use their washroom facilities. But one night, the air raid sirens didn't go off, but a bomb fell-- that was the first warning of an air raid-- across the street and smashed all the windows in the neighborhood. And we flew down those steps to the basement. And by the way, Jews were not allowed to sit in the basement air raid shelters with the non-Jews. But those people, our neighbors, insisted that we do. And there was never any sign of discomfort for us being there. We were one of them. We were their neighbor. My mom and dad were tremendously respected in this town. My mother, they call her a saint to this day, those that have survived. Whenever a Catholic child across the street-- we lived across the street from the Catholic church-- whenever a Catholic child was unable to afford a communion dress, she would furnish it. When the church needed new carpeting, she would give it to them without charge. She just took care of everybody. And they loved her for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: A very generous woman.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was a very generous, saintly woman. So they did not retaliate because we were Jews. They talked about Hitler and how awful things were, and what's going to happen, the neighbors. And the maid, she also couldn't work for us anymore. She cried. She was heartbroken. She didn't want to leave. And Mother told her, you've got to go now, Gertrude.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your experiences with your immediate neighbors were of good, kind people.

speaker: interviewee
text: The ones that I remember, yes. I'm sure my mother probably had some Nazi attitudes somewhere along the lines. But I didn't experience it myself. Now, when we were getting ready to move, we didn't know where we would go. I also had to give up my cat. And Gertrude took the cat with her to her home. She lived in a little tiny town. And we visited Gertrude once. I remember spending an afternoon calling, Prince, Prince, Prince. I was looking all for my Prince and he didn't show. I was very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how were the arrangements made for you to leave [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Well, Mr. Kindervater said to try England. Then the war with England broke out. So that closed that door. The next one he suggested was he was looking into passages to all these places with Russia. We were supposed to go through Russia to eventually get out. But somehow, with the relationship between Germany and Russia, it switched from they were allies to enemies. And when they'd shift to a place, that door closed. And fortunately, we weren't caught.

speaker: interviewer
text: So finally, finally, finally, we were called to Stuttgart, to-- that we were able to get out through Alfred's help here. Without Alfred, we wouldn't have been here. So it's a good thing he left when he did. We have to thank him for that. Your brother sponsored you and your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. He was in this country working as a painter-- not his own business. He had to work from scratch. He worked for a big Chicago firm. He worked there for many years until he started his own business. And he and Joe eventually got together and had another family business started in Coronado in California. Yes. Alfred, with his help, we were able to come. And at that time, I said good bye to all the kids at the Jewish school. And it was very painful to say good bye to Aunt Martha.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any last words she had for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wrote them in my little album. They were very poetic-- to always remember and to have hope. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah. And I had to say good bye to little Hans Egan. He was my only little buddy. My mother had to say good bye to his mother. They became very good friends. After the war, they corresponded again. And when I came back, many years later, Hans Egan had a surprise for me-- in his basement, he kept a bundle like this of letters that my mother wrote and my [INAUDIBLE] my writing. He gave me a couple of them to take home. Yeah. Anyway, I say good bye to Hans Egan. And the-- the Poles had a maid. Her name was Gertrude. No, no, that was my maid. Agnes-- and Agnes, I used to look up at her. And she was bigger than I was. She was an adult. But she was only 19. When I came back, I was looking down at little Agnes. She wasn't so big. But we all loved each other very, very much. They were decent people. I think without them, we couldn't have made it-- with the rations and the companionship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somehow, those good relationships must have given you faith in humanity.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I mean, we saw ugliness, and we had tremendous fear. But not all the people were like that.
